title: Pallet Calculator
tagline: Calculate boxes per pallet, stacking layers, total weight, and storage efficiency for logistics and warehousing.
intro: Enter pallet and box dimensions, weight limits, and stacking constraints to optimize your pallet loading for shipping and warehousing.

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive numbers for all required fields.
error.zeroHeight: Box height and max stack height must be greater than zero.

about.heading: About the Pallet Calculator
about.body:
Efficient pallet loading is one of the most impactful ways to reduce shipping costs, maximize warehouse space, and protect goods during transit. Whether you manage a distribution centre, coordinate freight shipments, or design packaging, knowing exactly how many boxes fit on a pallet — and how much that load weighs — is fundamental to daily operations.

A pallet is a flat transport structure that supports goods in a stable fashion while being lifted by a forklift, pallet jack, or front loader. The most common standard pallet in North America is the GMA (Grocery Manufacturers Association) pallet at 48 × 40 inches. In Europe, the Euro-pallet (EPAL) measures 1200 × 800 mm. ISO standard pallets come in six sizes. Knowing your pallet type and its maximum load rating is the starting point for any loading calculation.

The calculation proceeds in three steps. First, the floor footprint: divide the pallet length by the box length to find how many boxes fit lengthwise; divide the pallet width by the box width to find how many fit widthwise. Take the integer (floor) of each division and multiply them together to get the boxes per layer. This assumes boxes are aligned without rotation — for more complex optimization involving box rotation or mixed orientations, specialized software may be needed.

Second, the height constraint: divide the maximum stack height by the box height and take the floor to find how many layers can be stacked without exceeding the height limit. Height limits often come from warehouse racking systems, truck trailer height clearance (typically 98–110 inches interior), or stability requirements.

Third, the weight constraint: divide the maximum pallet weight by the weight of a single box to find the maximum number of boxes the pallet can carry. Divide that by boxes per layer to get the maximum number of layers by weight. The actual number of layers is the smaller of the height-limited and weight-limited values.

Final outputs: total boxes per pallet = boxes per layer × total layers; total load weight = total boxes × box weight. Space utilization = (total box volume / pallet stack volume) × 100%.

Proper pallet loading improves transport efficiency by reducing the number of trips and pallet positions needed, lowers damage rates by preventing shifting and overhangs, and ensures compliance with freight carrier weight limits and warehouse storage rules. This calculator provides a quick first estimate; always validate with your logistics team and verify compliance with carrier specifications before shipping.

examples.heading: Pallet Loading Examples
examples.intro: Typical pallet loading scenarios for common box sizes and standard GMA pallets.

items.input: 48×40 in pallet, 12×10×8 in boxes, 60 in height, 25 lb/box, 2000 lb max
items.note: 2,000 lbs — weight is limiting factor (5 layers vs. 7 by height)

items.input: 48×40 in pallet, 16×12×10 in boxes, 60 in height, 40 lb/box, 2500 lb max
items.note: 2,160 lbs — height and weight both allow 6 layers

items.input: 48×40 in pallet, 8×8×6 in boxes, 48 in height, 10 lb/box, 1500 lb max
items.note: 1,500 lbs — weight is limiting factor (5 layers vs. 8 by height)

items.input: 48×40 in pallet, 24×20×12 in boxes, 72 in height, 50 lb/box, 2000 lb max
items.note: 1,200 lbs — height is limiting factor (6 layers vs. 10 by weight)

items.input: 48×40 in pallet, 12×8×10 in boxes, 60 in height, 30 lb/box, 1800 lb max
items.note: 1,800 lbs — weight is limiting factor (3 layers vs. 6 by height)

howto.heading: How to Use the Pallet Calculator
steps: Enter the Pallet Length and Pallet Width in inches (standard GMA pallet is 48 × 40 in).
Enter the Box Length, Box Width, and Box Height in inches for the boxes being loaded.
Enter the Max Stack Height in inches — the maximum height limit for the fully loaded pallet.
Enter the Box Weight in lbs and the Max Pallet Weight in lbs to enforce weight constraints.
Click Calculate to see boxes per layer, number of layers, total boxes, total weight, and space utilization.

items.a: The most common pallet in North America is the GMA (Grocery Manufacturers Association) pallet, 48 × 40 inches. European EPAL pallets measure 1200 × 800 mm (approximately 47.2 × 31.5 inches). Always confirm the pallet dimensions used by your carrier or warehouse.

items.q: What is the maximum weight a standard pallet can hold?
items.a: A standard GMA wood pallet is typically rated for a static load of 2,500 to 3,000 lbs (1,100–1,360 kg). Dynamic load ratings (when moving on a forklift) are lower, often 1,500–2,000 lbs. Plastic and metal pallets have different ratings. Always verify the load capacity with the pallet manufacturer.

items.q: Why does this calculator use floor division for boxes per layer?
items.a: Floor division ensures only whole boxes are counted — you cannot place half a box. Any remaining space is considered overhang waste. For example, if the pallet is 48 inches and the box is 14 inches, floor(48/14) = 3 boxes fit, leaving a 6-inch gap.

items.q: Can I rotate boxes to fit more per layer?
items.a: This calculator assumes a single orientation (length along pallet length, width along pallet width). Rotating some boxes at 90° can sometimes increase the boxes per layer. For complex mixed-orientation loading, specialized pallet optimization software provides better results.

items.q: What is a typical maximum stack height?
items.a: Standard truck trailers have an interior height of about 98–110 inches. Warehouse racking typically constrains stack height to 48–72 inches for stability and forklift access. The maximum height also depends on the type of goods — fragile or crushable products require lower stacks.

items.q: How do I account for the pallet's own weight?
items.a: A standard wood pallet weighs roughly 30–70 lbs. If your carrier's weight limit applies to the total shipment (pallet plus cargo), subtract the pallet weight from the maximum load weight before entering it in the Max Pallet Weight field. This ensures your calculated load does not exceed the carrier limit.
